insights: All four human callers (ITIL User, Don Goodliffe, David Loo, Bud Richman) generated exactly 125 incident tickets each—indicating perfect volumetric balance with zero concentration risk across the caller population.

No caller exhibits a statistically significant increasing trend in incident volume over the 13-month period (all p-values > 0.05), directly refuting the premise of identifying 'the caller with increasing incident reports'.

Incident characteristics (category, location, priority) are uniformly distributed across all callers—with no significant associations (p > 0.50 for all chi-square tests) and normalized entropy >0.99—confirming structural consistency in reporting behavior, not differentiation.

David Loo shows the highest temporal volatility (CV = 36.83%, max/min ratio = 4.25) and a statistically notable spike in October 2023 (17 incidents, z = 2.08), representing the only short-term anomaly with operational relevance for monitoring or root-cause review.

Monthly caller concentration remains low overall (HHI range: 0.2506–0.3016), but four isolated high-magnitude deviations (|z| > 2.0) occurred across different callers and months—suggesting sporadic, non-systemic pressure points rather than sustained imbalance.

The dataset exhibits moderate aggregate temporal volatility (average CV = 28.49%), yet all observed fluctuations lack directional persistence or cross-caller synchronization—ruling out systemic demand surges or cascading operational stress.
summary: The analysis definitively shows there is no caller with increasing incident reports—none of the four human callers (ITIL User, Don Goodliffe, David Loo, Bud Richman) exhibit statistically significant upward trends in ticket volume over the 13-month period, and all have identical aggregate counts (125 each) and mean monthly rates (9.62). Incident characteristics are also uniformly distributed across callers, confirming consistent, non-discriminatory reporting behavior. While David Loo displays the most pronounced short-term volatility—including a notable spike in October 2023—this reflects an isolated anomaly rather than an emerging trend. The absence of structural, volumetric, or temporal imbalance indicates stable, well-distributed incident creation across the human caller base; the next focus should be validating whether this stability holds under expanded time horizons or additional contextual data (e.g., resolution outcomes, re-open rates, or service domain alignment).
